summary: C3 (Chrysler Comprehensive Compensation) was the payroll project at Chrysler that birthed Extreme Programming. Started in 1993 and rebooted under Kent Beck in 1996, it became the first project to combine all XP practices. The software went live in 1997 paying around ten thousand people, but new development stopped in 1999 and eventually reverted to COBOL. While C3's early success inspired XP's spread, its eventual cancellation demonstrates that XP is no guarantee of success. Fowler notes that most public analyses of C3 lack factual basis and contain misleading information.
key_points: C3 was rebooted under Kent Beck in 1996, becoming the birthplace of Extreme Programming by combining practices Kent had used on previous projects
The system successfully went live in 1997 paying ~10,000 people before being canceled in 1999 and eventually reverted to COBOL
C3's early success inspired other projects to adopt XP, which continued XP's development even after C3's cancellation
Most public analyses of C3, including Wikipedia, are misleading and not based on actual knowledge of the project
Many C3 team members went on to successful XP work, including one notable VeryLowDefectProject
money_quotes: C3's cancellation, however, also proves that XP is no guarantee of success.
C3's early success was a great inspiration for XP, and C3 had an important role in blazing the trail for XP.
By the time the project was canceled, enough other projects had replicated C3's early success to continue XP's development.
I haven't seen any analysis based on much knowledge of the facts.
In particular the entry in Wikipedia is misleading and incomplete, much of its comments seem to be based on a paper from a determined XP critic whose sources are unclear.
key_insight: C3's significance lies not in its ultimate success or failure, but in being the crucible where Extreme Programming practices were first combined—and its cancellation reminds us that no methodology guarantees project success.
